sections.heading: Users of account authorised to consent on behalf of relevant
sections.title: Users authorized to consent on behalf of account holders
sections.summary: This section establishes that individuals authorized to consent on behalf of relevant account holders can act as their representatives for consent purposes. The authorization must be specific to the account type and must not be a general delegation. This applies to account holders who are unable to consent directly themselves.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies who can legally consent on behalf of account holders, which is relevant for determining consent validity in cross-border data transfers involving account-related information.

requirements.text: Users of account authorised to consent on behalf of relevant

sections.source_quote: Users of account authorised to consent on behalf of relevant

sections.heading: The day on which this Act receives the
sections.title: Effective date of the Act
sections.summary: This section specifies the effective dates for various provisions of the Act, including the day the Act receives Royal Assent and the 120-day period after that date. It details when different sections and schedules come into effect, with specific dates provided for the Act's commencement.

requirements.text: The day on which this Act receives the Royal Assent. 12 December 2003
On the day after the end of the period of 120 days beginning on the day on which this Act receives the Royal Assent. 10 April 2004

sections.source_quote: The day on which this Act receives the Royal Assent. 12 December 2003

sections.heading: The day on which this Act receives the (cont.)
Compilation
sections.title: Civil penalties for contraventions of civil penalty provisions
sections.summary: Section 25(4) states that subsections (1) and (3) of the Spam Act 2003 are civil penalty provisions. These provisions establish pecuniary penalties payable for contraventions, with proceedings for recovery instituted in the Federal Court. The court may also order compensation to victims and payment to the Commonwealth for financial benefits attributable to contraventions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the legal framework for civil penalties and compensation in cases of contraventions, relevant to DTIA assessments for financial institutions handling unsolicited commercial electronic messages.

requirements.text: Subsections (1) and (3) are civil penalty provisions
Pecuniary penalties are payable for contraventions of civil penalty provisions
Proceedings for recovery of penalties are to be instituted in the Federal Court
The Federal Court may direct payment of compensation to a victim of a contravention
The Federal Court may direct payment to the Commonwealth of an amount up to financial benefits attributable to contraventions

sections.source_quote: Subsections (1) and (3) are civil penalty provisions. Pecuniary penalties are payable for contraventions of civil penalty provisions. Proceedings for the recovery of penalties are to be instituted in the Federal Court. The Federal Court may make ancillary orders: (a) directing the payment of compensation to a victim of a contravention of a civil penalty provision; and (b) directing the payment to the Commonwealth of an amount up to the amount of any financial benefit that is attributable to a contravention of a civil penalty provision.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Performance injunctions for non-compliance with obligations
sections.summary: Section 35(2) outlines conditions under which the Federal Court may grant performance injunctions. The court can exercise this power if a person has refused or failed to comply with an act or thing, or if it is likely they will refuse or fail to comply, even if they have previously refused or failed. The court may also consider imminent danger of substantial damage.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details when performance injunctions can be granted, relevant for DTIA assessments involving third-party compliance with data transfer obligations.

requirements.text: The Federal Court may grant an injunction requiring a person to do an act or thing
The court may exercise this power if the person has refused or failed to do that act or thing
The court may exercise this power if it appears likely the person will refuse or fail to do that act or thing

sections.source_quote: The power of the Federal Court to grant an injunction requiring a person to do an act or thing may be exercised: (a) if the Court is satisfied that the person has refused or failed to do that act or thing—whether or not it appears to the Court that the person intends to refuse or fail again, or to continue to refuse or fail, to do that act or thing; or (b) if it appears to the Court that, if an injunction is not granted, it is likely that the person will refuse or fail to do that act or thing—whether or not the person has previously refused or failed to do that act or thing and whether or not there is an imminent danger of substantial damage to any person if the person refuses or fails to do that act or thing.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Enforceable undertakings for commercial electronic messages
sections.summary: Section 37 allows a person to give the ACMA an enforceable undertaking regarding commercial electronic messages or address-harvesting software. The ACMA may accept such undertakings, and the person may withdraw or vary them with consent. The ACMA can apply to the Federal Court for enforcement if a breach occurs.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides a mechanism for enforceable undertakings related to unsolicited commercial electronic messages, relevant for DTIA assessments involving third-party data transfers.

requirements.text: A person may give the ACMA an enforceable undertaking in connection with commercial electronic messages or address-harvesting software
The ACMA may accept a written undertaking for the purposes of this section
The person may withdraw or vary the undertaking at any time with ACMA consent

sections.source_quote: A person may give the ACMA an enforceable undertaking in connection with a matter relating to: (a) commercial electronic messages; or (b) address-harvesting software.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Formal warnings for civil penalty contraventions
sections.summary: Section 41 authorizes the ACMA to issue formal warnings when a person contravenes a civil penalty provision. This mechanism serves as a warning and potential enforcement step for non-compliance with the Spam Act 2003.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables formal warnings for contraventions, relevant for DTIA assessments when handling unsolicited commercial electronic messages.

requirements.text: The ACMA may issue a formal warning if a person contravenes a civil penalty provision

sections.source_quote: The ACMA may issue a formal warning if a person contravenes a civil penalty provision.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Designated commercial electronic messages
sections.summary: Section 43 defines when an electronic message is a designated commercial electronic message. This includes messages authorized by government bodies, political parties, charities, educational institutions, and specific conditions related to the recipient's enrollment status and the message's relevance to goods or services.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies conditions for designated commercial electronic messages, relevant for DTIA assessments involving third-party data transfers and compliance with consent requirements.

requirements.text: An electronic message is a designated commercial electronic message if the sending is authorized by a government body, registered political party, or registered charity
The message relates to goods or services
The body is the supplier or prospective supplier of the goods or services

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of this Act, an electronic message is a designated commercial electronic message if: (a) the sending of the message is authorised by any of the following bodies: (i) a government body; (ii) a registered political party; (iii) a registered charity; and (b) the message relates to goods or services; and (c) the body is the supplier, or prospective supplier, of the goods or services concerned.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Infringement notices for civil penalty contraventions
sections.summary: This Schedule establishes a system for issuing infringement notices as an alternative to Federal Court proceedings for civil penalty contraventions. An authorised ACMA person may issue an infringement notice if they have reasonable grounds to believe a person committed one or more contraventions within the past 12 months. The notice must include the recipient's name, the authorised person's name, brief details of the alleged contraventions, a payment deadline, payment instructions, and other specified matters.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines the procedural requirements for issuing infringement notices, which could impact data transfer assessments if the notices involve cross-border data sharing or processing of personal information.

requirements.text: An authorised person may give an infringement notice if they have reasonable grounds to believe a person committed one or more contraventions of a civil penalty provision
An infringement notice must be given within 12 months after the day on which the civil contraventions are alleged to have taken place
An infringement notice must set out the name of the person to whom the notice is given
An infringement notice must set out the name of the authorised person who gave the notice
An infringement notice must either set out brief details of each alleged civil contravention or be accompanied by data processing devices containing brief details
An infringement notice must contain a statement that the matter will not be dealt with by the Federal Court if the penalty is paid within 28 days or a longer period allowed by ACMA
An infringement notice must give an explanation of how payment of the penalty is to be made
An infringement notice must include the date of the alleged contravention and the civil penalty provision allegedly contravened for each contravention

sections.source_quote: The object of this Schedule is to set up a system of infringement notices for contraventions of civil penalty provisions as an alternative to the institution of proceedings in the Federal Court.
